steps.title: Fastest path: mark the figure first
steps.content: Mark the given angle relationships on the figure first: equal angles, vertical angles, supplementary angles, parallel-line angles, and triangle sums of $180^\circ$. Most of these questions become a short angle chase once the givens are marked.

steps.title: Find angle K in radians
steps.content:
Angle J = $\frac{7\pi}{11}$ radians. Angle K is 4 times angle J:
$$K = 4 \times \frac{7\pi}{11} = \frac{28\pi}{11} \text{ radians}$$

steps.title: Convert angle K to degrees
steps.content:
To convert radians to degrees, multiply by $\frac{180}{\pi}$:
$$K = \frac{28\pi}{11} \times \frac{180}{\pi} = \frac{28 \times 180}{11}$$
The $\pi$ cancels. Factor 28 = 7 × 4:
$$K = \frac{7}{11} \times 180 \times 4$$

steps.title: Match to the answer choices
steps.content:
• A — $\frac{7}{11}(90)(4)$: uses 90 instead of 180. The radian-to-degree factor is 180/π, not 90/π. Wrong.
  • B — $\frac{7}{11}(180)(4)$: this is exactly $\frac{7}{11} \times 180 \times 4$, matching our derivation. That matches the correct answer — B is correct.
  • C — $\frac{7}{11\pi}(90)(4)$: places π in the denominator instead of canceling it, and uses 90. Both wrong.
  • D — $\frac{7\pi}{11}(180)(4)$: keeps π in the numerator; the π should cancel during conversion. Wrong.
